trajectory_summary: The call was disqualified at 02:44 on a hard DQ (renter, not homeowner), but the structural failure happened much earlier. Meg never established legitimacy, skipped the verify section entirely, and spent the middle of the call pitching a financially distressed, skeptical non-homeowner. The trajectory moved in one direction: cold → suspicious → disengaged → irritated, with no energy built at any point.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as unknown. The correct classification is hard_DQ — Justin is a renter, which is a non-homeowner disqualifier per dq_rules.not_homeowner. There is no appointment, no tentative booking, and no recoverable lead. The call should be tagged DQ_not_homeowner, not left as unknown.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, have you ever looked at solar? Do you know much about the program itself?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Meg skips the verify section entirely after the form denial and pivots to interest-probing. The renter DQ that ends the call at 02:44 is a direct consequence. On this call, the verify skip cost roughly 105 seconds of pitch time on an ineligible lead and left Justin more agitated than when the call started. The manager should drill verify.address_homeowner_confirmed as the mandatory first move after any legitimacy challenge — not interest-probing, not pitch. One question ('And you own the home there?') would have ended this call cleanly at 01:00.

key_moments.quote: Is there someone else in the home who maybe would've filled it out?
key_moments.stewart_read: When Justin denies the form, Meg probes for another household member. The alternative hypothesis — that this is a strategic indirect legitimacy attempt, trying to find a path back to a valid lead premise — is worth considering. But the evidence cuts against it: Meg doesn't follow the probe with any reframe of the call's purpose, and when Justin says no, she simply moves on. The more supported read is reactive: Meg is trying to preserve the lead premise because she has no legitimacy-recovery move ready. A strategic probe would have been followed by a pivot ('Either way, since I have you — can I take 30 seconds to tell you why I'm calling?'). That pivot never comes.

key_moments.quote: Well, have you ever looked at solar? Do you know much about the program itself?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the root-cause moment of the call. Meg skips the verify section entirely and pivots to interest-probing. The first required verify item — confirm address, home type, ownership — would have surfaced Justin's renter status in under 60 seconds. Instead, Meg spends the next 105 seconds pitching an ineligible lead. The skip is not strategic; there is no later verify attempt. It is a clean protocol omission.

Justin discloses financial hardship directly and plainly. Meg's response — 'that's one of the nice things about our program here is there's nothing upfront or out of pocket to go solar' — contains zero empathy acknowledgment before the pitch pivot. The line 'Nah. I feel you there.' at 01:11 is Justin's own voice, not Meg's; Meg offers no equivalent. This is a hard empathy miss: a vulnerability disclosure met with an immediate product feature. Even on a call that was already heading toward DQ, the absence of a human acknowledgment before the pivot is a trust-destroying move.

key_moments.quote: I cannot pay even. I cannot pay even. Just for free.
key_moments.stewart_read: Justin repeats his financial distress with more urgency — 'I cannot pay even. I cannot pay even.' This is a second vulnerability disclosure, escalating the first. Meg's response is to clarify that it's not free and explain the bill-replacement model. The explanation is technically accurate but again contains no empathy beat. At this point Justin has disclosed financial hardship twice in 12 seconds and received two consecutive pitch pivots. The pattern compounds the trust deficit.
